commentaries.author: Ambrosiaster
commentaries.content: The law said: “Love your neighbor and hate your enemy,” but the Lord says: “Unless your righteousness exceeds the righteousness of the scribes and the Pharisees, you will not enter the kingdom of God.” Therefore, in order for this to be the case, it is taught that we should not repay evil with evil. Commentary on Paul’s Epistles.

commentaries.author: Cyprian of Carthage
commentaries.content: In the Epistle of Paul to the Romans: "Rendering to no man evil for evil."

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: If you find fault with someone who is plotting against you, why expose yourself to the same accusation? Homilies on Romans

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: Paul means this: As far as possible, play your part and give nobody, either Jew or Gentile, any cause for fighting. But if you see the faith suffering anywhere, do not put harmony above truth. Make a noble stand, even to the point of death. And even then, do not be at war in your soul or of an adverse temper, but concentrate on the things themselves.

commentaries.author: Tertullian of Carthage
commentaries.content:
"Recompense to no man evil for evil.".
Conjoins. And the precept is absolute, that evil is not to be repaid with evil.
(That) we all know; provided, however, we remember what the same (God) has said through the apostle: "Let your probity appear before men.".
Has now grown old, ever since "Let none render evil for evil"

methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
